source_evidence: Section 4.3, Eq. (13): dx = L/N; r_F = (1/2)sqrt(2 dx^2) = sqrt(2)L/(2N); phi_F = atan((L/2)/r_F) = atan(sqrt(2)N/2); R = sqrt((L/2)^2 + r_F^2) = (L/2N)sqrt(N^2+2). 'where dx is the dimension of a single hexahedral element, r_F is the distance from the pole to the loaded nodes, and R is the radius of the (optimal) sphere.'
purpose: Computes the geometric quantities (element size, load-ring radius, moment latitude, optimal sphere radius) for approximating the torsion ball with a regular orthogonal cube mesh of N elements per side, explaining why phi_F changes with discretization.
assumptions: Regular structured-orthogonal cube mesh of dimension L discretized with N hexahedral elements in each direction
Loads and supports applied at the four nodes closest to the poles
Load ring radius r_F is the diagonal half-distance of one element from the pole
As N increases, phi_F increases (load ring shrinks), making the problem harder to approximate
R (13) exceeds the domain boundaries when 2R > L, so a cube cannot fully accommodate the optimal sphere
formula_or_algorithm: dx = L/N; r_F = (1/2) sqrt(2 dx^2) = sqrt(2) L / (2 N); phi_F = atan((L/2)/r_F) = atan(sqrt(2) N / 2); R = sqrt((L/2)^2 + r_F^2) = (L/(2N)) sqrt(N^2 + 2).
validation_opportunities: cross-check against tabulated R and phi_F values (Table 4)
limit check (as N grows, phi_F -> pi/2)

reuse_risk.explanation: Elementary trigonometric/geometric relations specific to the orthogonal-cube discretization of the ball problem; easily reproduced.
